text: The robotic surgical procedure focuses on a left inguinal hernia repair, where the sigmoid colon is visibly involved, forming part of the hernia sac wall as a colon slider.

The patient has a scrotal hernia where you could feel stool concretions within the colon in the hernia sac. These concretions are about the size of a golf ball. The hernia definitely is not reducible on physical exam, even with the patient relaxed. After induction of anesthesia, I was hoping that it would be reducible, but it still wasn't.

Inside the abdomen, the surgeon attempts to reduce the scrotal hernia with assistance from a bedside assistant, who applies external pressure while the surgeon pulls internally. Despite efforts, the hernia remains irreducible. The surgeon uses four ports, including one in the right lower quadrant, to facilitate the procedure. A high peritoneal flap is created to ensure ample working space and accommodate a large piece of mesh if necessary.

The surgeon navigates the thin peritoneum and enters the retro-rectus space to create a robust flap, ensuring it does not rip or shred during the reduction of the large hernia. Laterally, the surgeon maintains proximity to the peritoneum and visceral space.

The surgeon cuts across the posterior rectus sheath, which extends very low in this patient, to join the retro-rectus space with the lateral visceral preperitoneal space. A wide flap is created, and the left medial umbilical ligament is divided. Care is taken to divide the posterior rectus sheath rather than the linea alba to avoid causing an incisional hernia.

The surgeon crosses the midline into the contralateral retrorectus space, using the fourth robotic arm to retract the flap and facilitate two-handed dissection. The transverse salus sling and surrounding tissue are divided during this process.

The surgeon carefully identifies the plane between the testicular vessels and the peritoneum in the hernia sac, ensuring to cauterize on the superficial side of the tissue to avoid damaging the bowel wall. The dissection progresses under a safe plane closer to the peritoneum.

The surgeon encounters firm connective tissue likely due to the colon sliding in and out of the internal ring, causing fibrosis. The surgeon carefully hugs the peritoneum to free more of the hernia, using the fourth arm to pull harder. The transverse alias sling is visible as the surgeon continues to separate it from the hernia sac, aiming to pull fat and blood vessels off the sac to confirm the correct plane.

The surgeon navigates around the hernia site, identifying and preserving the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve and a visible femoral branch. The testicular vessels appear adhered to the hernia sac, and the surgeon explores for a potential plane between the hernia contents and lateral structures.

The surgeon identifies the colon forming the wall of the hernia and attempts to find the plane between the testicular vessels and the hernia sac. Fatty tissue is being separated from the hernia contents, likely revealing the testicular vessels. The surgeon uses a Cartier instrument while retracting the bowel.

The surgeon uses a Cartier instrument to handle the bowel within the hernia sac, noting that excessive pulling causes tissue to slip through the instrument. The dissection is focused on the anterior region, with the surgeon using cautery aggressively to divide fibrous tissue. The inferior epigastric vessels are a concern, while the vas deferens is noted to be more posterior than expected.

The surgeon divides the medial scar tissue within the indirect space, which allows for further dissection down the hernia sac. The hernia sac remains adhered to surrounding structures, requiring careful manipulation.

The surgeon continues to progress in the dissection, working to reduce the peripheral attachments of the hernia sac. The white edge of the direct inguinal hernia, referred to as the counter sac, is visible. The surgeon maintains this plane while ensuring minimal bleeding and plans to locate the testicular vessels later.

The surgeon maintains hemostasis while attempting to reduce the colon, which remains partially adhered to the hernia sac. Progress is made as the sac is further freed, making the hernia contents more reducible.

The surgeon identifies and reduces stool concretions from the hernia sac, pulling with maximum tension without tearing the tissue. A large piece of stool is visible as it pops through the internal opening. The colon remains part of the hernia sac, requiring further reduction of the sac to fully reduce the colon. The surgeon prepares to cauterize, aiming to remove fat and a small cord lipoma from the hernia sac, which should not contain much fat or blood vessels.

The surgeon identifies the testicular vessels posteriorly after reducing the bulk of the hernia sac. The testicular vessels are separated from the hernia sac, and the surgeon carefully dissects in the plane between them to avoid bleeding. Fat is removed from the anterior side of the hernia sac, while the posterior side contains bowel and colon mesentery, which cannot be fully defatted.

The surgeon identifies a band obstructing the hernia's reduction and uses cautery near the bowel, carefully lifting tissue away to avoid damage. The plane between the testicular vessels and the colon is visible, and the surgeon grasps the testicular vessels despite typically avoiding this due to associated autonomic nerve sensations.

The surgeon explores the fibrotic tissue of the hernia sac, using cautery to dissect and remove excess fat while protecting the inferior epigastric vessels. The hernia appears to have reduced, allowing focus on identifying the plane between the testicular vessels and the sac, and locating the tip of the sac.

The surgeon uses a spreading technique to pull tissue towards the nine o'clock position, aiming to expose the edge of the peritoneum. A large hernia sac is visible, with fatty tissue and vessels being carefully peeled off to reveal a white sac. The testicular vessels have already been separated from the sac.

The surgeon identifies a plane between the testicular vessels and the remaining colon, aiming to fully remove the hernia sac. The edge of the sac is visible, and the surgeon considers transecting the sac, noting that most of the bowel has been reduced and is no longer stuck in the inguinal canal.

The surgeon separates the testicular vessels from the hernia sac, revealing the leading edge of the sac. Fibrotic tissue is visible, characteristic of chronic hernias. The bedside assistant aids in reducing contents, while a third instrument through the fourth port provides retraction, allowing the surgeon's hands to work together. Larger vessels are identified and divided using bipolar cautery.

The surgeon identifies enlarged vessels supporting blood flow to the hernia sac and carefully dissects around the sac, maintaining distance from the vas deferens. The fibrotic tissue surrounding the sac is visible, and the plane between the peritoneum and testicular vessels is clearly defined.

Using a zero-degree scope, the surgeon navigates the large scrotal inguinal hernia, carefully dissecting layer by layer around the sac until the vas deferens is visible. The surgeon lifts tissue away from critical structures like the iliac vein, encountering significant scar tissue. The bladder is more challenging to separate due to the scar tissue.

The surgeon works to obtain a critical view of the myopectineal orifice, crossing the midline and reaching two centimeters deep into the retropubic space. A large piece of mesh, measuring seventeen by twelve centimeters, is prepared for use in repairing the large inguinal hernia.

The surgeon navigates through a dense network of vessels, ensuring hemostasis to avoid bladder injury. They work on the medial aspect, dividing an obstructive vessel. The transition from the retropubic space to the psoas muscle is refined to allow the mesh to sit flush against the superior pubic rami. Good retraction and tension facilitate safe dissection.

The surgeon skeletonizes the testicular vessels and vas deferens to ensure the mesh can be securely tucked beneath the hernia contents and colon. Care is taken to avoid injuring the iliac vein by pulling away and identifying the areolar plane between the bladder fat and the fat overlying the iliac vein.

The dissection is complete, revealing a bare space of Bogros laterally. The lateral femoral cutaneous nerve and the femoral branch are visible behind a shiny layer of tissue, indicating that the dissection did not go too superficial.

The surgeon positions the mesh medially, aligning the long line parallel to the allopubic tract and the vertical short line on the vas deferens. The mesh is tucked into the space of Retzius with good posterior lateral coverage to prevent recurrence. A 3-0 absorbable suture is placed superior laterally, and another stitch is added in Cooper's ligament for secure positioning.

The surgeon deploys a large mesh that covers the surgical area effectively. A third stitch is placed to secure the mesh, followed by closing the peritoneal flap. The wide mouth sac, containing a significant portion of colon, is left untouched to prevent bowel entrapment.

The surgeon takes large bites of the hernia sac to remove redundancy, using a Cartier to hold the sac up for efficient closure. The lower flap is manipulated to reduce the size of the large hernia sac.

The surgeon desufflates the abdomen to four millimeters mercury to evacuate CO₂ during flap closure. After closing the flap, the abdomen is re-insufflated to fifty millimeters mercury to ensure the peritoneum presses against the mesh, preventing the posterior edge from lifting. The mesh is securely tucked posteriorly with the sac and colon positioned on top.

The surgeon repairs a small hole in the peritoneum, ensuring it is pushed up against the hernia mesh. The lattice imprint of the mesh is visible, and the mesh is tucked in nicely, with a slight curl laterally that is not expected to cause recurrence.
